content_en:
Adh-Dhahabi's theological orientation was Athari-Hanbali, and this positioning shaped both his biographical evaluations and his own theological contributions. His relationship with Ibn Taymiyyah — whose bold challenges to received Ash'ari positions adh-Dhahabi admired, while also expressing reservations about some of Ibn Taymiyyah's more controversial stances — illustrates the complexity of his theological position.

In Siyar A'lam an-Nubala, adh-Dhahabi's theological views appear most clearly in his evaluations of scholars whose theological positions he considered deviant. Mu'tazili scholars receive clear note of their theological problems alongside acknowledgment of their scholarly achievements in other areas. Philosophers who mixed Islamic theology with Greek philosophy in ways adh-Dhahabi considered problematic receive appropriately critical treatment.

His treatment of Ash'ari scholars is more nuanced: as the dominant theological school of his era's scholarly establishment, the Ash'aris occupied a position that adh-Dhahabi could not simply dismiss, but his own Athari leanings meant that he was aware of differences between Ash'ari ta'wil and what he considered the salaf's approach of affirmation without interpretation.

For Sufi scholars, adh-Dhahabi's evaluations reflect a similarly nuanced position: he respected the genuine spiritual dimension of Islamic piety and acknowledged the learning of many Sufi scholars, while being critical of doctrines and practices he considered unsupported by the Quran and Sunnah or that seemed to approach pantheism.

His treatment of his own contemporary Ibn Taymiyyah in Siyar A'lam an-Nubala and in his personal letters provides some of the most interesting material in the biographical tradition: an admiring student who was also an independent scholar capable of noting where he thought his teacher had gone too far or taken untenable positions. This combination of loyalty and intellectual independence is characteristic of the best biographical scholarship. For students of Islamic intellectual history, adh-Dhahabi's treatment of the Athari, Ash'ari, and Sufi traditions in Siyar A'lam an-Nubala provides an invaluable window into how a senior scholar of impeccable Sunni credentials navigated the theological diversity within the tradition — neither collapsing all distinctions in the name of unity nor drawing the boundaries of acceptable Sunnism so narrowly as to exclude significant portions of the scholarly heritage.
content_id:
Orientasi teologis adz-Dzahabi adalah Athari-Hanbali, dan posisi ini membentuk baik evaluasi biografinya maupun kontribusi teologisnya sendiri. Hubungannya dengan Ibn Taymiyyah — yang tantangan-tantangan beraninya terhadap posisi-posisi Asy'ari yang diterima dikagumi adz-Dzahabi, sementara beliau juga menyatakan keberatan atas beberapa sikap Ibn Taymiyyah yang lebih kontroversial — mengilustrasikan kompleksitas posisi teologisnya.

Dalam Siyar A'lam an-Nubala, pandangan teologis adz-Dzahabi muncul paling jelas dalam evaluasinya atas ulama-ulama yang ia anggap posisi teologisnya menyimpang. Ulama-ulama Mu'tazilah mendapat catatan jelas tentang masalah-masalah teologis mereka di samping pengakuan atas pencapaian ilmiah mereka di bidang-bidang lain. Para filsuf yang mencampur teologi Islam dengan filsafat Yunani dengan cara yang adz-Dzahabi anggap bermasalah mendapat perlakuan yang tepat-kritikal.

Perlakuannya terhadap ulama-ulama Asy'ari lebih bernuansa: sebagai mazhab teologi dominan dari lingkungan ilmiah eranya, kaum Asy'ariyyah menempati posisi yang tidak bisa adz-Dzahabi abaikan begitu saja, tetapi kecenderungan Athari-nya sendiri berarti bahwa beliau sadar akan perbedaan antara ta'wil Asy'ari dan apa yang ia anggap sebagai pendekatan salaf berupa penetapan tanpa interpretasi.

Untuk ulama-ulama Sufi, penilaian adz-Dzahabi mencerminkan posisi yang sama bernuansa: beliau menghormati dimensi spiritual yang tulus dari kesalehan Islam dan mengakui keilmuan banyak ulama Sufi, sementara kritis terhadap doktrin-doktrin dan praktik-praktik yang menurutnya tidak didukung oleh Al-Qur'an dan Sunnah atau yang tampak mendekati panteisme.

Perlakuannya terhadap Ibn Taymiyyah, rekan zamannya sendiri, dalam Siyar A'lam an-Nubala dan dalam surat-surat pribadinya memberikan materi paling menarik dalam tradisi biografi: seorang murid yang mengagumi yang juga merupakan ulama independen yang mampu mencatat di mana menurutnya gurunya telah pergi terlalu jauh atau mengambil posisi yang tidak dapat dipertahankan. Kombinasi kesetiaan dan kemandirian intelektual ini menjadi ciri khas beasiswa biografi terbaik. Bagi pelajar sejarah intelektual Islam, perlakuan adz-Dzahabi atas tradisi-tradisi Athari, Asy'ari, dan Sufi dalam Siyar A'lam an-Nubala memberikan jendela yang tak ternilai tentang bagaimana seorang ulama senior dengan kredensial Sunni yang sempurna menavigasi keragaman teologis dalam tradisi tersebut — tanpa meruntuhkan semua perbedaan atas nama persatuan maupun menarik batas-batas Sunnisme yang dapat diterima begitu sempit sehingga mengecualikan bagian-bagian signifikan dari warisan ilmiah.
content_ar: Adh-Dhahabi's theological orientation was الأثري-الحنبلي, and this positioning shaped both his biographical evaluations and his own theological contributions. His relationship with ابن تيمية — whose bold challenges to received الأشعري positions adh-Dhahabi admired, بينما أيضاً expressing reservations about some of ابن تيمية's more controversial stances — illustrates the complexity of his theological position. In Siyar A'lam an-Nubala, adh-Dhahabi's theological views appear most clearly in his evaluations of العلماء whose theological positions he considered deviant. Mu'tazili العلماء receive clear note of their theological problems alongside acknowledgment of their العلمي achievements in other areas. Philosophers who mixed Islamic theology with Greek philosophy in ways adh-Dhahabi considered problematic receive appropriately البالغ الأهمية treatment. His treatment of الأشعري العلماء is more nuanced: as the dominant theological school of his era's العلمي establishment, the Ash'aris occupied a position that adh-Dhahabi could not simply dismiss, but his own الأثري leanings meant that he was aware of differences between الأشعري ta'wil and what he considered the السلف's approach of affirmation without interpretation. For Sufi العلماء, adh-Dhahabi's evaluations reflect a similarly nuanced position: he respected the genuine spiritual dimension of Islamic piety and acknowledged the learning of many Sufi العلماء, بينما being البالغ الأهمية of doctrines and practices he considered unsupported by the القرآن الكريم and السنة or that seemed to approach pantheism. His treatment of his own contemporary ابن تيمية in Siyar A'lam an-Nubala and in his personal letters provides some of the most interesting material in the biographical التراث: an admiring student who was أيضاً an independent scholar capable of noting where he thought his teacher had gone أيضاً far or taken untenable positions. This combination of loyalty and intellectual independence is characteristic of the best biographical scholarship. For students of Islamic intellectual history, adh-Dhahabi's treatment of the الأثري, الأشعري, and Sufi traditions in Siyar A'lam an-Nubala provides an invaluable window into how a senior scholar of impeccable Sunni credentials navigated the theological diversity within the التراث — neither collapsing all distinctions in the name of unity nor drawing the boundaries of acceptable Sunnism لذا narrowly as to exclude المهم portions of the العلمي heritage.
